content:
The idea of varying one quantity while holding others fixed appears in 18th-century work on **partial differential equations** in physics—Euler and others studied how fields change in space while time was held fixed, and vice versa.

**Euler** (1734) and later **Clairaut** formalized notation and symmetry of mixed partials for smooth functions. **Lagrange** and the calculus-of-variations tradition extended multivariable differentiation to optimization over functions.

In the 20th century, **functional analysis** and **automatic differentiation** research reframed partial derivatives as the local linear map (Jacobian entries) behind multivariable optimization.

With machine learning, partial derivatives became operational at industrial scale: each **∂L/∂θᵢ** in backpropagation powers training of classifiers, diffusion models and large language models. What began as slicing a surface along a coordinate axis is now computed billions of times per training step on GPU clusters.

timeline.label: Multivariable calculus (18th c.)
timeline.description: Euler, Clairaut and contemporaries develop partial derivative notation and symmetry theorems for smooth functions.

timeline.label: PDEs and variational methods
timeline.description: Physics and optimization formalize rates of change with several independent variables.

timeline.label: Automatic differentiation era
timeline.description: Backpropagation and autograd frameworks compute partials of composed graphs efficiently for neural network training.
